coredata.dc:title: Construction of novel S/CdS type II heterojunction for photocatalytic H2 production under visible light: The intrinsic positive role of elementary α-S
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Meiyu
Lin, Haili
Cao, Jing
Guo, Xiaomin
Chen, Shifu
coredata.dc:description: Traditional view points that the photocorrosion product sulfur clusters have negative effect on the activity of CdS system. Differently, in this paper, little amount of crystalline elementary α-sulfur (α-S) nanoparticles were loaded on the surface of CdS nanorods and resulted in enhanced photocatalytic H2 production activity under visible light (λ>420nm). The photocatalytic H2 production over 10% S/CdS composite was 8.14mmol and further rose to 30.02mmol in the presence of K2PtCl6 after 3h irradiation. In the novel S/CdS system, largely improved separation efficiency of photoexcited charges were achieved through the heterojunction interface role between α-S nanoparticles and CdS nanorods. The matching type II energy band structure ensures that α-S could quickly trapped electrons from the host CdS nanorods for reducing H+ to produce H2. After 10 cycles experiment, S/CdS still kept excellent photocatalytic H2 production activity. This study clearly demonstrates the intrinsic promoting effect of α-S in CdS photocatalyst even in other metal sulfide systems for enhancing photocatalytic H2 production activity under visible light.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalytic H2 production
Co-catalyst
Elementary α-S
Metal sulfide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: H2 production over novel S/CdS composites
H2 production over novel S/CdS in the presence of K2PtCl6
Stability of S/CdS under visible light
The role of α-S in the novel S/CdS system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of pure CdS, α-S and S/CdS composites.
SEM images of pure CdS, α-S and S/CdS composites, and TEM images of 5%, 10%, 15% and 20% S/CdS composites.
TEM and HRTEM patterns of 10% S/CdS composite photocatalyst.
(a) XPS survey spectra of CdS, α-S and S/CdS composites. High resolution XPS spectra of (b) Cd 3d, (c) S 2p and (d) enlarged S 2p spectra of 20% S/CdS.
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of CdS and S/CdS composites.
(a) The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of CdS, α-S and S/CdS composites, (b) the corresponding band gap energies (E g) of CdS and α-S and (c) Mott-Schottky curves of CdS and α-S.

(a) Photocatalytic H2 evolution and (b) the average H2 evolution rate over the as-prepared photocatalysts under visible light (λ>420nm), (c) comparison of H2 evolution rate over 10% S/CdS and the physical mixture of α-S and CdS under visible light (λ>420nm).
(a) Transient photocurrent spectra, (b) electrochemical impendence spectra, (c) photoluminescence spectra and (d) surface photovoltage spectra of the as-prepared samples.

(a) Photocatalytic H2 evolution and (b) the average H2 evolution rate over the as-prepared photocatalysts in the presence of K2PtCl6 (2.56μmol) under visible light (λ>420nm), (c) comparison of H2 evolution rate over 10% S/CdS and the physical mixture of α-S and CdS in the presence of K2PtCl6 (2.56μmol) under visible light (λ>420nm).

(a) Recycling test of 10% S/CdS sample for photocatalytic H2 evolution in the presence of K2PtCl6 (2.56μmol) under visible light (λ>420nm), (b) XRD patterns, (c) XPS survey spectra, high resolution XPS spectra of (d) Cd 3d and (e) S 2p of the fresh and used 10% S/CdS.
Schematic diagram of the synthetic route of S/CdS composite.
Schematic illustrations of the mechanism over S/CdS composites under visible light (λ>420nm).

head.ce:title: Construction of novel S/CdS type II heterojunction for photocatalytic H2 production under visible light: The intrinsic positive role of elementary α-S

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science/Information College, Huaibei Normal University, Huaibei 235000, Anhui, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science/Information College

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Anhui Key Laboratory of Energetic Materials, Huaibei 235000, Anhui, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Anhui Key Laboratory of Energetic Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Structural Chemistry, Fuzhou 350002, Fujian, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Structural Chemistry

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Chemistry and Materials Science/Information College, Huaibei Normal University, Huaibei 235000, Anhui, PR China (J. Cao).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science/Information College

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Crystalline α-S was firstly introduced to CdS system for H2 production via a facile solvent evaporation-deposition-precipitation method. The novel S/CdS composites exhibited excellent H2 production activity and stability under visible light due to the intrinsic promoting effect of α-S on photocarriers.

Traditional view points that the photocorrosion product sulfur clusters have negative effect on the activity of CdS system. Differently, in this paper, little amount of crystalline elementary α-sulfur (α-S) nanoparticles were loaded on the surface of CdS nanorods and resulted in enhanced photocatalytic H2 production activity under visible light (λ>420nm). The photocatalytic H2 production over 10% S/CdS composite was 8.14mmol and further rose to 30.02mmol in the presence of K2PtCl6 after 3h irradiation. In the novel S/CdS system, largely improved separation efficiency of photoexcited charges were achieved through the heterojunction interface role between α-S nanoparticles and CdS nanorods. The matching type II energy band structure ensures that α-S could quickly trapped electrons from the host CdS nanorods for reducing H+ to produce H2. After 10 cycles experiment, S/CdS still kept excellent photocatalytic H2 production activity. This study clearly demonstrates the intrinsic promoting effect of α-S in CdS photocatalyst even in other metal sulfide systems for enhancing photocatalytic H2 production activity under visible light.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the global problems of energy crisis and environment pollution, the development of clean and renewable energy technologies has raised much more attention worldwide. Light-driven H2 evolution over semiconductors has been regarded as a promising challenge to convert solar energy to available energy because of its potential application in H2 economy [1–3].

Among various kinds of reported semiconductor photocatalysts, CdS is still considered to be one of the most effective H2 production photocatalysts working under visible light [4–7]. It has been widely recognized that CdS could achieve high H2 production activity through loading co-catalysts to improve the separation efficiency of photocarriers [8,9]. Except that, holes scavenger, such as Na2S/Na2SO3, is also a key factor to keep the activity and stability of CdS system [10–12]. On one hand, S2− and SO3 2− ions consume the photogenerated holes on the surface of CdS, which effectively inhibits the combination of photoexcited charges. On the other hand, SO3 2- ions also react with newly formed sulfur (S) clusters on the surface of CdS, avoiding the light absorption disturbance of S to CdS. However, in fact, the S clusters always exist in the CdS system in Na2S/Na2SO3 solution [11]. Thus, the traditional viewpoint is that S clusters have negative effect on the activity of CdS and must be eliminated immediately from the surface of CdS [13,14].

The recent studies revealed that, however, the yellow α-sulfur crystals of cycloocta sulfur (S8) could be deemed as a novel elementary photocatalyst [15]. In addition, the crystalline state α-S can also be combined with In2O3 [16] and BiOCl [17] to construct composites used for degrading organic pollutant. That is, the crystalline state α-S not only was applied as single phase photocatalyst but also could be acted as efficient co-catalyst to enhance the separation efficiency of photoexcited charges. Besides, the semiconductor characteristics of other elementary substances, such as Si [18,19], C [20–24], B [25] and P [26–29], were also investigated in detail in photocatalysis field. Considering the above analysis, whether the crystalline state α-S can be employed as highly efficient co-catalyst in CdS system? If so, it is expected that crystalline state α-S can quickly trap the electrons on the conduction band of CdS, which will improve the separation efficiency of photoexcited charges and finally enhance the photocatalytic H2 evolution performance of CdS. Moreover, the crystalline state α-S is quite stable in the Na2S/Na2SO3 solution, different from the fresh S clusters. Therefore, it is urgent and important to elucidate the intrinsic role of crystalline state α-S in CdS system. However, to the best of our knowledge, no results have been reported on the enhancement effect of crystalline state α-S in CdS system.

In the present study, we loaded α-S nanoparticles on the surface of CdS nanorods via a special solvent evaporation-deposition-precipitation method for the first time. The as-prepared novel S/CdS composites displayed enhanced photocatalytic H2 production performance under visible light. The role of α-S in S/CdS system was discussed on the basis of transient photocurrent, electrochemical impendence, photoluminescence and surface photovoltage measurements. The significant finding of this study breaks through the traditional understanding of S in metal sulfide system and provides new insight to the positive role of crystalline α-S as stable and highly efficient co-catalyst.

All chemicals with analytical purity were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. and were used without further purification. Deionized water was employed in all experiments.
(1)
Synthesis of CdS nanorods via solvothermal process. Typically, 15.11mmol Cd(NO3)2·4H2O was dissolved in 40mL of ethylenediamine, then 32mL of ethylenediamine solution including 45.32mmol thiourea was added into the Cd(NO3)2 solution drop by drop. The above dispersion was transferred to a 100mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 160°C for 48h. Finally, the autoclave was cooled down naturally and the yellow precipitate was collected, washed with deionized water and ethanol, and dried at 60°C in air.
(2)
Construction of S/CdS composite through facile solvent evaporation-deposition-precipitation method. 2.08mmol CdS was homogeneously dispersed in 10mL ethanol. After that, 2mL CS2 solution containing 0.94mmol S was dropwise put into the above CdS dispersion. At room temperature, the solvent was evaporated gradually until the end with vigorous stirring. The final product was defined as 10% S/CdS (The weight percent of S to CdS was 10%). Similarly, 5%, 15% and 20% S/CdS composites were also synthesized. The whole preparation process of S/CdS was displayed in the following Scheme 1 . For comparison, pure crystalline state α-S was obtained in the absence of CdS.

The crystal structure of the samples was investigated using a BRUKER D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer with a scanning speed of 10°/min and Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.5406Å). The morphology of the samples was investigated on an FEI Sirion 200 field emission scanning electron microscope with 5.00kV scanning voltages. TEM and HRTEM images were acquired on a JEOL-2011 transmission electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 200kV. XPS data were collected on a Thermo ESCALAB 250 with Al Kα (1486.6eV) line at 150W. BET specific surface area of the powders was analyzed by a Micromeritics ASAP2020 apparatus at liquid nitrogen temperature (77.3K). The optical properties of the samples were studied using a TU-1901 UV–vis spectrophotometer in the range of 200–800nm wavelength. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured on a JASCO FP-8300 spectrofluorometer with 365nm excitation wavelength.

The transient photocurrent was investigated on an electrochemical analyzer (CHI660E, Chenhua Instruments Co. Shanghai, China) equipped with a standard three-electrode including a working electrode, a Pt wire as a counter electrode and Ag/AgCl (3.0M KCl) as a reference electrode. A 300W Xe lamp (CEL-HXF 300, Beijing China Education Au-light co., Ltd.) with a 420nm cutoff filter was used as light source. Phosphate buffered saline contained 0.1M Na2HPO4 and 0.1M NaH2PO4 aqueous solution was applied as the electrolyte meanwhile indium-tin oxide (ITO) glass was chosen as the working electrode. In a typical procedure, 0.05g photocatalyst was dispersed in 1.0mL of chitosan under sonication for 20min to produce homogeneous slurry. Then, the slurry was covered on the conductive surface of ITO glass to generate a solid film with an area of 1.00cm2 and sintered in air at 80°C for 2h. The Mott-Schottky plot was collected under the same conditions without the Xe lamp irradiation. Electrochemical impendence spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out in a 0.2M KCl solution.

The surface photovoltage (SPV) spectroscopy was measured on a TLS-SPV530 spectrometer (Zolix instruments Co., Beijing, China). Monochromatic light was obtained by passing light from a 500W Xe lamp through a double prism monochromator with 1mm slit width of entrance and exit. A lock-in amplifier, synchronized with a light chopper was employed to amplify the photovoltage signal. The range of modulating frequency was from 20 to 70Hz and the spectral resolution was 1nm.

The photocatalytic H2 production experiments were performed on a photocatalytic hydrogen production system (CEL-SPH2N, Beijing China Education Au-light co., Ltd.). In a typical photocatalytic reaction, 50mg of photocatalyst was dispersed in 30mL of aqueous solution containing 0.75mol·L−1 Na2S and 1.05mol·L−1 Na2SO3 as sacrificial reagents. The gases in the reaction system were pulled out with a vacuum-pumping system and the reactor was kept at 6°C by using a circular cooling water system. Then, the photocatalyst suspension was irradiated with a 300W Xe lamp with a 420nm cut-off filter under magnetic stirring. The illumination time was controlled within 3h and the concentration of H2 evolved was analyzed by a gas chromatograph (GC-7900) using a 5Å molecular sieve column and a thermal conductivity detector (TCD).

Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of S/CdS samples with different α-S loading contents. Pure CdS had sharp XRD peaks and corresponded to the hexagonal phase structure of CdS (JCPDS file No. 65-3414). Moreover, the single α-S was of orthorhombic structure (JCPDS file No. 08-0247) with high crystallinity. Utilizing solvent evaporation-deposition-precipitation method, little amount of α-S was loaded on the surface of CdS, which can be ensured by the XRD patterns of 5–20% S/CdS composites. With increasing the loading amount of α-S, the (222) peak intensity of α-S strengthened gradually while the peak intensity of CdS decreased correspondingly. Due to the low contents of loaded α-S, its peak intensity was not high in S/CdS composites.

Fig. 2 exhibits the morphologies of the as-synthesized samples. As can be seen, the pristine CdS was of regular two dimensional nanorods structure with length of 50–200nm (Fig.2a). Since CdS nanorods were modified by low amount of α-S nanoparticles, the resulting S/CdS composites still showed the similar morphology to pure CdS nanorods. However, the surface of CdS nanorods became rough with increasing the amount of α-S (Fig.2b–e). The corresponding TEM images of 5–20% S/CdS (Fig.2g–j) display that only little amount of α-S nanoparticles were loaded on the surface of CdS nanorods, suggesting that the effective loading amount of α-S nanoparticles was very low. In addition, the pure α-S presented a large aggregate structure with irregular morphology (Fig.2f).

The TEM and HRTEM analysis were further applied to investigate the microstructure of the S/CdS, the corresponding images of the 10% S/CdS sample are shown in Fig. 3 . It can be seen from Fig.3a and b that, CdS nanorods were coated with trace amount of α-S nanoparticles. The HRTEM image in Fig.3c further displays that there are two types of lattice fringes that closely contacted with each other. One had interplanar distance of 0.117nm, was in accordance with the (301) lattice plane of CdS. The other one was 0.162nm, indexed to the (624) plane of α-S. This sufficiently ascertains that the α-S nanoparticles tightly adhered to the CdS nanorods, which results in the successful formation of S/CdS heterojunction interface through the solvent evaporation-deposition-precipitation method. It is expected that the S/CdS interface will play crucial role for the separation of photoexcited charges under visible light.

The surface chemical composition of all the samples was measured by XPS technology. The survey XPS spectra (Fig.4 a) demonstrate that there are Cd, S, C and O elements in CdS and S/CdS samples, while only S, C and O elements were discovered in pure α-S. Generally speaking, the peaks of O and C may originate from gaseous molecules adsorbed on the surface of photocatalyst and adventitious hydrocarbon adsorbed on XPS equipment, respectively. It is observed from the high resolution XPS spectra of Cd 3d (Fig.4b) that the peaks located at 405.3 and 412.1eV were assigned to Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2, respectively, suggesting the valence state of Cd element was of +2 in CdS and S/CdS samples [30,31]. As for the S 2p spectra in Fig.4c, it can be splitted into two different peaks for CdS but three peaks for S/CdS samples. In detail, The peak located at 162.6eV corresponded to the S 2p1/2 and another one situated around 161.4eV was ascribed to S 2p3/2 [32,33]. Additionally, the binding energy distance between S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 was 1.2eV, further indicating the presence of the chemical state of S2− in CdS [29]. Especially, the weak peak with binding energy of 163.6eV could be ascribed to be zero valence elementary S [16], originating from the surface loaded low content of α-S in the 5–20% S/CdS composites. The enlarged S 2p peak in 20% S/CdS sample was selected and presented in Fig.4d. Based on the XPS results, the calculated contents of loaded α-S were 1.40%, 1.44%, 1.46% and 1.49% for 5%, 10%, 15% and 20% S/CdS composites.

To explore the surface areas and textural properties, all of the synthesized photocatalysts were measured by N2 adsorption-desorption process at 77.3K. Fig. 5 shows that all of the samples had the similar typical type IV N2 adsorption-desorption characteristic [34,35] according to the IUPAC classification. Furthermore, each isotherm displays an obvious H3 hysteresis loop in a relative pressure range of 0.9–1.0, indicating that mesopores were generated though the aggregation of CdS nanorods [36]. As a result, the BET specific surface areas (S BET) of CdS, 5–20% S/CdS composites were 27.844, 23.327, 22.518, 20.559 and 15.697m2 g−1, respectively. The S BET values of samples decreased slowly with increasing the α-S loading contents, which is probably due to the adhesion of CdS nanorods via α-S nanoparticles. Differently, the pure α-S had very small S BET value of 0.006m2 g−1, owing to its very large particle size.

The optical properties of the as-prepared photocatalysts were investigated by the UV–vis absorption spectra. In Fig.6 a, the absorption edge of pure CdS located at about 518nm while that of pure α-S situated at around 460nm. Even though, the low content of loaded α-S on the surface of CdS nanorods could not obviously decrease the light absorption of CdS, which guarantees the excellent visible light utilization of S/CdS composites.

Moreover, the corresponding band gap energies (E g) of the CdS and α-S were estimated by the following formula (1) [37]. The result was provided in Fig.6b.
(1)
α hv = A
( hv -
E
g
)
n / 2
where α, h, ν, A and E g stand for absorption coefficient, Planck constant, light frequency, a constant and band gap energy, respectively. The value of n is a constant determined by the type of optical transition of a semiconductor, including direct transition (n =1) and indirect transition (n =4). As reported, both CdS [38] and α-S [16] belonged to direct band semiconductors with the corresponding n value of 1.

On the basis of the Eq. (1), the E g values of the samples can be inferred from the tangent line of curve depicted in Fig.6b. Consequently, the fitting results showed that the E g values of CdS and α-S were about 2.41 and 2.79eV, respectively, which agrees well with the values reported previously [39,40].

In order to further calculate the conduction band potential (E CB) and valance band potential (E VB) of CdS and α-S, their corresponding Mott-Schottky curves were measured in detail [41,42]. Fig.6c shows that both CdS and α-S belonged to n-type semiconductors [43] owing to their positive slopes of the C−2-E. As a result, the flat band potential (Vfb) equaled −0.72V and −0.37V vs. Ag/AgCl at pH 6.8 for CdS and α-S, respectively, via extrapolating the Mott-Schottky curves. In other words, the above Vfb values were −0.52V and −0.17V vs. NHE at pH 6.8 [44]. According to literatures, the E CB value of an n-type semiconductor is very close to its Vfb value [44]. So, the E CB values of CdS and α-S approximately equaled to be −0.52V and −0.17V vs. NHE at pH 6.8. Based on the following equation E CB = E VB − E g, the E VB values of CdS and α-S were estimated to be 1.89V and 2.62V. It can be seen that, the energy band structure of S/CdS composite can be regarded as typical type II interactive structure, which facilitates the separation efficiency of photoexcited charges under visible light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: H2 production over novel S/CdS composites
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the role of α-S in S/CdS system, the H2 production activity of S/CdS composites was investigated in a mixed Na2S/Na2SO3 aqueous solution under visible light (λ>420nm). It can be seen (Fig.7 a) that pure CdS nanorods could generate 5.08mmol H2 after 3h irradiation while no any H2 could be detected over individual α-S. It is exciting that the little α-S on the surface of CdS nanorods remarkably improve the H2 production activity of CdS. For example, the 10% S/CdS produced 8.14mmol H2 after 3h irradiation. In addition, the loading amount of α-S obviously affected the yield of H2 over S/CdS system. Namely, the activity of S/CdS firstly increased and then decreased oppositely, in which the 10% S/CdS displayed the best activity in all of the samples.

For comparison, the average photocatalytic H2 evolution rates over all the samples were also calculated and the results were shown in Fig.7b. Clearly, 10% S/CdS gave the highest H2 evolution rate of 2.71mmol·h−1·g−1, which was about 1.60 times of that obtained over pure CdS. The above results distinctly exhibited the positive role of α-S acting as an efficient co-catalyst in S/CdS system. In general, the interface between the host and the co-catalyst is the key factor as for the separation of photoexcited charges. Thus, the physical mixture of CdS and α-S was also applied to investigate the H2 production activity under visible light (λ>420nm). The corresponding result was displayed in Fig.7c. It declares that the photocatalytic activity of the simple mixture of CdS and α-S was slightly higher than that of pure CdS but much weaker than that of S/CdS. This result testifies that S/CdS composite but not the simple physical mixture was constructed through facile solvent evaporation-deposition-precipitation method.

Apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) is an important factor to reflect the photon utilization in the photocatalytic process. On the basis of the Eqs. (2) and (3) [45,46], the AQE of 10% S/CdS composite at 420nm was calculated as follows.
(2)
AQE =
2 × n (
H
2
) ×
N
0
N ( photon )
× 100 %
(3)
N ( photon ) = F × S × t
where n(H2), N 0 and N(photon) represent mole number of evolved H2, Avogadro constant and number of incident photons, respectively. Meanwhile, F, S and t refer to the power of the light, light radiation area and illumination time. In our experiment, n(H2)=1.29×10−4 mol, N 0 =6.022×1023, F =3.37×1020 Photon·m−2·s, S =2.12×10−3 m2 and t =7200s. Therefore, the AQE value of 10% S/CdS composite at 420nm was calculated to be 3.03%.

Generally speaking, the enhanced activity of semiconductor originated from the improved separation of photoexcited charges [47–49]. Considering the extensive application of photoelectrochemical property of semiconductor, the transient photocurrent curves and electrochemical impendence spectroscopy (EIS) were measured to account for the photoexcited charges separation efficiency of the samples. It is widely accepted that high peak intensity suggests high photocarriers separation efficiency [50–54]. As shown in Fig.8 a, both CdS and α-S displayed very weak transient photocurrent response under visible light (λ>420nm), which indicates they had fast recombination ratio of photocarriers. After little α-S was loaded on the surface of CdS nanorods, the photocurrent response of S/CdS dramatically improved. The change in the photocurrent intensity was accordance with that of photocatalytic H2 production activity under visible light. The EIS curves of the samples in Fig.8b also suggest that the little loaded α-S effectively decreased the impendence of CdS nanorods although the single α-S had very large impendence.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were also widely used to illustrate the photoexcited charges separation efficiency. The low PL intensity suggests the high separation efficiency of photocarriers [36,55]. As shown in Fig.8c, CdS sample displayed the highest PL intensity, indicating the lowest separation efficiency of photocarriers. The decreased PL intensity caused by the loading of α-S resulted from the enhanced separation efficiency of photocarriers. Similar to the EIS characterization in Fig.8b, the change in PL intensity of the S/CdS composites depended on the loading amount of α-S. 10% S/CdS had the lowest PL response, indicating it possessed the highest separation efficiency of photocarriers.

Moreover, the charge transfer direction was also detected by surface photovoltage (SPV) spectra to further illustrate the separation pathway of photocarriers [56–58]. After light irradiation, the photogenerated electrons and holes moved oppositely and resulted in different SPV signals. It can be seen from Fig.8d, the positive SPV signals indicate that the photogenerated holes transferred to the surface meanwhile electrons migrated to the inner of the photocatalyst samples [59]. Pure CdS nanorods showed very strong SPV intensity, which suggests quantity of holes moved to the surface and then aggregated. That is, only little amount of electrons reached the surface of CdS nanorods to further take part in the H+ reduction reaction. Single α-S had very weak SPV response due to its fast recombination of photoexcited charges. The existence of α-S also intensively affected the SPV response of CdS nanorods. The obvious characteristic is that the SPV intensity of S/CdS composites decreased in comparison to that of CdS nanorods. Namely, plenty of electrons moved to the surface of S/CdS and then decreased the number of net charges. This result mainly rooted in the excellent electron trapping role of α-S nanoparticles. When 10% α-S was loaded, the S/CdS composite possessed the lowest SPV intensity. However, extra of α-S nanoparticles (>10%) yet increased the SPV intensity of 15% and 20% S/CdS compared with that of 10% S/CdS composite. It probably that the large sized α-S nanoparticles weakened the electron trapping ability. Furthermore, much more holes generated from α-S and transferred to the surface of CdS nanorods, which leads to the enhancement of SPV intensity. The abovementioned results sufficiently proved that surface α-S nanoparticles markedly strengthened the photoexcited charges separation efficiency of CdS, which endows enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution of S/CdS composites under visible light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: H2 production over novel S/CdS in the presence of K2PtCl6
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared S/CdS composites were further evaluated for photocatalytic H2 evolution in the presence of K2PtCl6 as electron capturer [60–62] under visible light (λ>420nm). As shown in Fig.9 a and b, 10% S/CdS composite could produce 30.02mmol H2 after 3h irradiation and the corresponding H2 evolution rate was 10.01mmol·h−1·g−1. It is 5.92 and 1.99 times of H2 evolution rate of single CdS without and with K2PtCl6. This result suggests that the positive role of α-S in CdS system can be further amplified in the presence of K2PtCl6. Similarly, the closely contacted S/CdS interface was the premise to acquire high activity of H2 evolution, ensured by the result in Fig.9c.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of S/CdS under visible light
ce:section.ce:para: Stability is crucial to the practical application of photocatalysts, so the activity change was observed in the successive use procedure. Fig.10 a exhibits that 10% S/CdS sample maintained excellent photocatalytic H2 evolution about 28.23mmol after 10 cycles test. The corresponding XRD (Fig.10b) and XPS (Fig.10c–e) patterns of the fresh and used 10% S/CdS samples were also measured. As shown in Fig.10b, the XRD peak intensity and position of 10% S/CdS before and after photocatalysis were not prominently changed. This ensures the high stability of S/CdS. Furthermore, the XPS patterns of the used 10% S/CdS had the same characteristic as the fresh one except that the molar ratio of S to CdS improved from 1.44% to 1.45% according to the Fig.10c. Briefly, loading little α-S on the surface of CdS could effectively depress the damage of photocorrosion and improve the photocatalytic activity of CdS photocatalyst. It is expected that S/CdS system can be considered as a promising composite used in photocatalytic H2 evolution under visible light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The role of α-S in the novel S/CdS system
ce:section.ce:para: From the above results and analysis, it can be concluded that little content of α-S in CdS system indeed enhanced the photocatalytic H2 evolution activity through improving the separation efficiency of photocarriers. Hence, it is quite natural to analyze the photocarriers separation mechanism, illustrated in Scheme 2 , on the basis of the energy band structure of S/CdS composite [63–65]. When little content of α-S was loaded on the surface of CdS nanorods, the novel S/CdS heterojunction interface was formed. Due to the matching energy band structure between α-S and CdS, quantity of electrons located on the conduction band of CdS nanorods would quickly transfer to that of α-S, meanwhile a small number of holes on the valence band of α-S moved oppositely to that of CdS nanorods under visible light. Consequently, the photocarriers of host CdS were efficiently separated. The electrons aggregated on the conduction band of α-S reduced H+ to H2 while the holes gathered on the valence band of CdS nanorods were consumed by Na2S/Na2SO3. The highly efficient separation efficiency of photoexcited charges assures the high activity of S/CdS composites. In the photocatalytic process, the α-S mainly acted as effective co-catalyst and played intrinsic positive role for enhancing the activity of CdS. It should be noted that, the crystalline α-S was stable enough in Na2S/Na2SO3 solution, which keeps the high activity and stability of novel S/CdS composites.

In this paper, we successfully constructed a series of novel S/CdS composites through a facile solvent evaporation-deposition-precipitation method. Although α-S had bigger E g value than that of CdS, the low surface loading contents of α-S ensured the excellent visible light absorption of S/CdS composites. Under visible light, the novel S/CdS displayed largely enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution activity, which results from the high separation efficiency of photocarriers through the tightly touched S/CdS heterojunction interface. The positive role of α-S in CdS system was verified via transient photocurrent, electrochemical impendence, photoluminescence and surface photovoltage measurements. The present work confirms that the earth-abundant α-S has tremendous potential as an efficient co-catalyst to strongly promote photocatalytic H2 production activity of metal sulfides.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (51472005, 51272081), the Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (1708085MB32), the Natural Science Foundation of Educational Committee of Anhui Province (gxyqZD2016413, gxyqZD2016414, KJ2015A027), State Key Laboratory of Structural Chemistry (20160014) and Innovation Team of Design and Application of Advanced Energetic Materials.
